audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Deuteronomy / Devarim debt-release, poverty, servant-release, and firstborn law, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for seventh-year release, lending ethics, poverty contradiction, open-hand command, anti-hardheartedness warning, Hebrew servant liberation gifts, voluntary permanent service, and firstborn animal consecration.

verses.restored: At end of seven years you shall make a release.
This is matter of release: every creditor shall release what he lent to his neighbor; he shall not press his neighbor or his brother, because release to YHWH has been proclaimed.
The foreigner you may press, but what is yours with your brother your hand shall release.
Yet there should be no poor among you, for YHWH will surely bless you in land YHWH your Divine gives you as inheritance to possess,
if only you diligently listen to voice of YHWH your Divine, keeping to do all this command I command you today.
For YHWH your Divine will bless you as he spoke to you; you shall lend to many nations but shall not borrow, and rule over many nations but they shall not rule over you.
If there is among you a poor person, one of your brothers, within one of your gates in your land YHWH your Divine gives you, you shall not harden your heart or shut your hand from your poor brother.
But you shall surely open your hand to him and surely lend him enough for his lack, whatever is lacking to him.

Guard yourself lest there be a worthless word in your heart, saying, Seventh year, year of release, is near, and your eye is evil toward your poor brother and you give him nothing; he may cry to YHWH against you, and sin/misalignment will be in you.
You shall surely give to him, and your heart shall not be grieved when you give, because for this thing YHWH your Divine will bless you in all your work and in all undertaking of your hand.
For poor will not cease from midst of land; therefore I command you, saying, You shall surely open your hand to your brother, to your poor and needy in your land.
If your brother, a Hebrew man or Hebrew woman, is sold to you, he shall serve you six years, and in seventh year you shall send him free from you.
When you send him free from you, you shall not send him empty.
You shall surely furnish him from your flock, threshing floor, and winepress; from what YHWH your Divine blessed you, you shall give to him.
You shall remember you were a slave in land of Egypt and YHWH your Divine redeemed you; therefore I command you this thing today.
It shall be, if he says to you, I will not go out from you, because he loves you and your house, because it is good for him with you,
then you shall take awl and put it through his ear into door, and he shall be your servant permanently; and also to your female servant you shall do likewise.
It shall not be hard in your eyes when you send him free from you, for double value of hired worker he served you six years; and YHWH your Divine will bless you in all you do.
Every firstborn male born in your herd and flock you shall sanctify to YHWH your Divine; you shall not work with firstborn of ox or shear firstborn of flock.
Before YHWH your Divine you shall eat it year by year in place YHWH chooses, you and your household.
If there is in it blemish—lame or blind, any bad blemish—you shall not sacrifice it to YHWH your Divine.
Within your gates you shall eat it; unclean and clean together, like gazelle and deer.
Only its blood you shall not eat; upon earth you shall pour it like water.

translation_notes: The seventh-year release interrupts indefinite debt pressure within the covenant community.
Verse 4's vision that there need be no poor stands beside verse 11's realism that poverty persists; the tension intensifies rather than cancels the open-hand command.
The permission to press a foreigner reflects unequal ancient in-group/out-group economics and must not justify predatory lending or xenophobia.
The poor person's actual lack determines the response; giving is not limited to symbolic charity.
Hebrew servants, male and female, are released with productive assets because Exodus memory forbids liberation into destitution.
Permanent ear-piercing service remains an ancient dependency structure and must not be romanticized as consent free from economic pressure.
Disability language about sacrificial blemish concerns altar eligibility, not the worth of disabled humans or animals.
safety_note: Do not use Deuteronomy 15 to defend slavery, debt bondage, xenophobic finance, coerced service, or disability stigma. Center debt release, open-handed provision, liberation with resources, and the Exodus demand that freedom must be materially survivable.
